title: PID with Tubo-Ovarian Abscess
chief_complaint: I have severe lower abdominal pain, fever, and a foul-smelling yellow vaginal discharge for 5 days. It hurts during sex and when I pee. I have an IUD.

physical_exam.Abdomen: Bilateral lower quadrant tenderness. Guarding. No rebound (yet).
physical_exam.Pelvic: Purulent cervical discharge. Cervical motion tenderness (exquisite). Bilateral adnexal tenderness and fullness (right > left). Uterus tender. IUD strings visible.

hidden_data.ct_abdomen: Bilateral tubo-ovarian abscesses (Right 6cm, Left 4cm) with complex fluid collections, thick enhancing walls. Inflammatory fat stranding. Free fluid in pelvis. IUD in correct position.
hidden_data.cervical_cx: Cervical cultures: Chlamydia trachomatis PCR positive. Neisseria gonorrhoeae PCR negative.
hidden_data.hiv: HIV test (should be offered to all PID patients)

correct_diagnosis: Severe Pelvic Inflammatory Disease with Bilateral Tubo-Ovarian Abscesses from Chlamydia trachomatis
key_learning_points: PID = ascending infection from lower genital tract to upper tract (endometritis, salpingitis, oophoritis, peritonitis). Most common: Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae. Often polymicrobial (anaerobes, GBS, Enteric Gram-negatives)

Risk factors: Age <25, Multiple sexual partners, Unprotected sex, Previous PID, IUD (first 3 weeks after insertion), Douching. IUD does NOT cause PID but increases risk during insertion period if undiagnosed infection present

Clinical: Lower abdominal pain (bilateral), Fever, Purulent cervical discharge, Cervical motion tenderness, Adnexal tenderness. CDC diagnostic criteria: Minimum (cervical motion + adnexal + uterine tenderness). Supportive: Fever >38.3, Purulent discharge, WBC >10K, ESR/CRP elevated, GC/Chlamydia positive

Tubo-ovarian abscess (TOA): Complication of PID (15%). Inflammatory mass involving tube, ovary, bowel, omentum. Requires prolonged antibiotics and often drainage. Ruptured TOA = surgical emergency (peritonitis, sepsis)

Treatment: CDC PID guidelines. INPATIENT criteria: TOA, Pregnancy, Severe illness (fever >38.5, WBC >15K, peritonitis), Failed outpatient therapy, Cannot tolerate PO, Immunocompromised. Antibiotics: Ceftriaxone 1g IV q24h + Doxycycline 100mg PO/IV BID + Metronidazole 500mg IV q8h. Alternative: Clindamycin + Gentamicin. Continue for 14 days total (transition to PO Doxycycline + Metronidazole when improving)

TOA management: Antibiotics alone resolve 70% of TOA <7cm. Percutaneous drainage (IR-guided) if >7cm, not improving in 48-72h, or enlarging. Surgical drainage (laparoscopy/laparotomy) if IR not feasible or ruptured. Salpingo-oophorectomy if destroyed adnexa. Remove IUD (CDC recommends removal but evidence weak). Partner treatment: Azithromycin 1g PO single dose (or Doxycycline 100mg BID ×7 days). Reportable disease. Counsel about sequelae: Infertility (20%), Chronic pelvic pain (30%), Recurrent PID (25%), Ectopic pregnancy (10× increased risk)
